content.claim: School-choice mechanisms in Hungary, including early academic selection, are empirically associated with increased ethnic segregation; towns with more pronounced school commuting patterns (enabled by free choice) show stronger inter-school inequalities and higher segregation between Roma and non-Roma students.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Cited studies examine school-choice segregation broadly rather than isolating the specific contribution of the 6/8-year gimnázium mechanism
The causal pathway from early tracking to segregation is inferred rather than directly measured in the source materials
Substantial heterogeneity exists across towns, and local policy effects are variable
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
